content: The equation of the circle which passes through the points of intersection of the circles $${x^2} + {y^2} - 6x = 0$$ and $${x^2} + {y^2} - 6y = 0$$, and has its centre at $$¥¥left( {{3 ¥¥over 2},{3 ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)$$ is
explanation:
Required equation of circle is

¥n

$${x^2} + {y^2} - 6x + ¥¥lambda ({x^2} + {y^2} - 6y) = 0$$ .... (1)

¥n

or, $$(1 + ¥¥lambda ){x^2} + (1 + ¥¥lambda ){y^2} - 6x - 6¥¥lambda y = 0$$

¥n

or, $${x^2} + {y^2} - {{6x} ¥¥over {(1 + ¥¥lambda )}} - {{6¥¥lambda y} ¥¥over {1 + ¥¥lambda }} = 0$$

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ Centre $$¥¥left( {{3 ¥¥over {1 + ¥¥lambda }},{{3¥¥lambda } ¥¥over {1 + ¥¥lambda }}} ¥¥right)$$

¥n

Putting $${3 ¥¥over {1 + ¥¥lambda }} = {3 ¥¥over 2}$$

¥n

$$1 + ¥¥lambda = 2$$ $$¥¥therefore$$ $$¥¥lambda = 1$$

¥n

Substituting $$¥¥lambda$$ = 1 in equation (1), we get

¥n

$${x^2} + {y^2} - 6x + {x^2} + {y^2} - 6y = 0$$

¥n

$${x^2} + {y^2} - 3x - 3y = 0$$.

content: The equation $$(x - {x_1})(x - {x_2}) + (y - {y_1})(y - {y_2}) = 0$$ represents a circle whose centre is
explanation:
$$(x - {x_1})(x - {x_2}) + (y - {y_1})(y - {y_2}) = 0$$

¥n

This is equation of circle in diameter form where $$({x_1},{y_1})$$ and $$({x_2},{y_2})$$ are the end points of the diameter.

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ Centre is midpoint of diameter $$ = ¥¥left( {{{{x_1} + {x_2}} ¥¥over 2},{{{y_1} + {y_2}} ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)$$.

¥n

content: The locus of the centres of the circles which touch both the axes is given by
explanation:
>

¥n

Let the coordinates of centre of circle are (h, k) and radius is a unit. Circle touches both the axes

¥n

h = a ..... (i)

¥n

k = a ..... (ii)

¥n

Eliminating a by squaring (i) and (ii) and then subtracting we get $${h^2} - {k^2} = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {x^2} - {y^2} = 0$$, which is required equation of locus.

content: The equation of the chord of he circle $${x^2} + {y^2} - 4x = 0$$ whose midpoint is (1, 0) is
explanation:
Equation of chord of circle

¥n

x2 + y2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, where midpoint is (x1, y1) is T = S1

¥n

where T = xx1 + yy1 + g(x + x1) + f(y + y1) + c

¥n

and S1 = x$$_1^2$$ + y$$_1^2$$ + 2gx1 + 2fy1 + c

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ Equation of chord of the circle x2 + y2 $$-$$ 4x = 0 whose midpoint is (1, 0) is

¥n

1x + 0y $$-$$ 2(x + 1) + 0(y + 0) = 12 + 02 $$-$$ 4 . 1

¥n

$$¥¥Rightarrow$$ $$-$$ x $$-$$ 2 = $$-$$ 3 $$¥¥Rightarrow$$ x = 1

content: The circles x2 + y2 $$-$$ 10x + 16 = 0 and x2 + y2 = a2 intersect at two distinct points if
explanation:
Two circles having centres C1, C2 and radii r1 and r2 are intersecting when

¥n

$$|{r_1} - {r_2}| < {C_1}{C_2} < {r_1} + {r_2}$$

¥n

Here, C1 $$¥¥equiv$$ (5, 0), C2 $$¥¥equiv$$ (0, 0),

¥n

$${r_1} = ¥¥sqrt {25 + 0 - 16} = 3$$, $${r_2} = a$$

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ $$|3 - a| < ¥¥sqrt {{{(5 - 0)}^2} + {{(0 - 0)}^2}} < 3 + a$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow |3 - a| < 5 < 3 + a ¥¥Rightarrow |3 - a| < 5$$ and $$5 < 3 + a$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow - 5 < 3 - a < 5$$ and $$a > 2 ¥¥Rightarrow - 2 < a < 8$$ and $$a > 2$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow 2 < a < 8$$.

content: For the two circles x2 + y2 = 16 and x2 + y2 $$-$$ 2y = 0 there is/are
explanation:
Centre C1 of x2 + y2 = 16 is (0, 0) and radius (r1) = 4

¥n

Centre C2 of x2 + y2 $$-$$ 2y = 0 is (0, 1) and radius (r2) = $$¥¥sqrt {{1^2} + 0 - 0} = 1$$

¥n

$${C_1}{C_2} = ¥¥sqrt {{{(0 - 0)}^2} + {{(0 - 1)}^2}} = 1$$

¥n

$${r_1} - {r_2} = 4 - 1 = 3$$

¥n

Since $${C_1}{C_2} < {r_1} - {r_2}$$.

¥n

So second circle lies completely inside the first circle, so there is no common tangent possible.

content: The straight line x + y $$-$$ 1 = 0 meets the circle x2 + y2 $$-$$ 6x $$-$$ 8y = 0 at A and B. Then the equation of the circle of which AB is a diameter is
explanation:
x + y $$-$$ 1 = 0 ...... (1)

¥n

x2 + y2 $$-$$ 6x $$-$$ 8y = 0 ....... (2)

¥n

Any circle passing through pts. of intersection of (1) & (2) is

¥n

$${x^2} + {y^2} - 6x - 8y + k(x + y - 1) = 0$$ ..... (3)

¥n

[S + K . L = 0 form]

¥n

i.e. $${x^2} + {y^2} + (k - 6)x + (k - 8)y - k = 0$$

¥n

If AB be a diameter of (3) then its centre $$¥¥left( {{{6 - k} ¥¥over 2},{{8 - k} ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)$$ will lie on (1)

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {{6 - k} ¥¥over 2} + {{8 - k} ¥¥over 2} - 1 = 0$$ $$¥¥therefore$$ k = 6

¥n

from (3), x2 + y2 $$-$$ 2y $$-$$ 6 = 0 is the required circle.

content: If the straight line y = mx lies outside of the circle x2 + y2 $$-$$ 20y + 90 = 0, then the value of m will satisfy
explanation:
y = mx lies outside of x2 + y2 $$-$$ 20y + 90 = 0

¥n

$$¥¥Rightarrow$$ perpendicular distance of y = mx from centre (0, 10) must be greater than radius $$ = ¥¥sqrt {{0^2} + {{10}^2} - 90} = ¥¥sqrt {10} $$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {{|m¥¥,.¥¥,0 - 10|} ¥¥over {¥¥sqrt {{m^2} - {{( - 1)}^2}} }} > ¥¥sqrt {10} $$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow 10({m^2} + 1) < 100 ¥¥Rightarrow {m^2} < 9 ¥¥Rightarrow |m| < 3$$

content: The locus of the centre of a circle which passes through two variable points (a, 0), ($$-$$a, 0) is
The intercept on the line y = x by the circle x2 + y2 $$-$$ 2x = 0 is AB. Equation of the circle with AB as diameter is
explanation:
Circle through points of intersection of

¥n

y = x ...... (i) &

¥n

x2 + y2 $$-$$ 2x = 0 ..... (ii) is

¥n

x2 + y2 $$-$$ 2x + k(x $$-$$ y) = 0 ...... (iii)

¥n

$$¥¥because$$ Centre of (iii) is $$¥¥left( {1 - {k ¥¥over 2},{k ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)$$

¥n

According to problem, $$¥¥left( {1 - {k ¥¥over 2},{k ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)$$ will lie on (i)

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ $${k ¥¥over 2} = 1 - {k ¥¥over 2}$$

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ k = 1

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ From (iii) : $${x^2} + {y^2} - x - y = 0$$, $$x(x - 1) + y(y - 1) = 0$$

content: If the coordinates of one end of a diameter of the circle $${x^2} + {y^2} + 4x - 8y + 5 = 0$$ is (2, 1), the coordinates of the other end is
explanation:
$$¥¥because$$ One end is (2, 1) & centre is ($$-$$2, 4)

¥n

Let other end be (x, y)

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ $${{x + 2} ¥¥over 2} = - 2$$ & $${{y + 1} ¥¥over 2} = 4$$

¥n

$$¥¥Rightarrow$$ coordinates is ($$-$$6, 7)

content: Let $x-y=0$ and $x+y=1$ be two perpendicular diameters of a circle of radius $R$. The circle will pass through the origin if $R$ is equal to
explanation:
The centre of the circle is the intersection of the two diameters

¥n

$$ ¥¥begin{aligned} & x-y=0 ¥¥¥¥ & x+y=1 ¥¥end{aligned} $$

¥n

Adding the equations gives $2 x=1 ¥¥Rightarrow x=¥¥frac{1}{2}$. substituting $x=¥¥frac{1}{2}$ into $x-y=0$ gives $¥¥frac{1}{2}-y=0$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow ¥¥quad y=¥¥frac{1}{2} $$

¥n

So, the centre of the circle is $¥¥left(¥¥frac{1}{2}, ¥¥frac{1}{2}¥¥right)$.

¥n

The circle passes through the origin $(0,0)$.

¥n

The radius $R$ is the distance between the center and the origin.

¥n

$$ ¥¥begin{aligned} R & =¥¥sqrt{¥¥left(¥¥frac{1}{2}-0¥¥right)^2+¥¥left(¥¥frac{1}{2}-0¥¥right)^2} ¥¥¥¥ & =¥¥sqrt{¥¥frac{1}{4}+¥¥frac{1}{4}}=¥¥sqrt{¥¥frac{2}{4}} ¥¥¥¥ & =¥¥sqrt{¥¥frac{1}{2}}=¥¥frac{1}{¥¥sqrt{2}} ¥¥end{aligned} $$

content: The number of common tangents to the circles $x^2+y^2-4 x-6 y-12=0, x^2+y^2+6 x+18 y+26=0$ is
explanation:
Given two circles

¥n

$$ C_1: x^2+y^2-4 x-6 y-12=0 $$

¥n

Centre $G_1=(2,3)$

¥n

$$ ¥¥text { } ¥¥begin{aligned}Radius¥¥,¥¥, R_1 & =¥¥sqrt{2^2+3^2-(-12)} ¥¥¥¥ & =¥¥sqrt{4+9+12}=¥¥sqrt{25}=5 ¥¥¥¥ C_2 & =x^2+y^2+6 x+18 y+26=0 ¥¥end{aligned} $$

¥n

Centre $G_2=(-3,-9)$

¥n

$$ ¥¥text { } ¥¥begin{aligned}Radius¥¥,¥¥, R_2 & =¥¥sqrt{(-3)^2+(-9)^2-26} ¥¥¥¥ & =¥¥sqrt{9+81-26}=¥¥sqrt{64}=8 ¥¥end{aligned} $$

¥n

The distance between the centres

¥n

$$ ¥¥begin{aligned} & d=¥¥sqrt{(2-(-3))^2+(3-(-9))^2} ¥¥¥¥ & d=¥¥sqrt{(5)^2+(12)^2}=¥¥sqrt{25+144}=¥¥sqrt{169}=13 ¥¥end{aligned} $$

¥n

The sum of the radii $R_1+R_2=5+8=13$.

¥n

Since $d=R_1+R_2$, the circles touch externally.

¥n

Therefore, there are 3 common tangents.

content: Chords $$¥¥mathrm{AB}$$ & $$¥¥mathrm{CD}$$ of a circle intersect at right angle at the point $$¥¥mathrm{P}$$. If the length of AP, PB, CP, PD are 2, 6, 3, 4 units respectively, then the radius of the circle is
If two circles which pass through the points $$(0, a)$$ and $$(0,-a)$$ and touch the line $$¥¥mathrm{y}=¥¥mathrm{m} x+¥¥mathrm{c}$$, cut orthogonally then
A curve passes through the point (3, 2) for which the segment of the tangent line contained between the co-ordinate axes is bisected at the point of contact. The equation of the curve is
explanation:
>

¥n

According to the question, p(x, y) is the midpoint of line AB.

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ $${{¥¥alpha + 0} ¥¥over 2} = x ¥¥Rightarrow ¥¥alpha = 2x$$

¥n

$${{¥¥beta + 0} ¥¥over 2} = y ¥¥Rightarrow ¥¥beta = 2y$$

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ Point A = (2x, 0)

¥n

and Point B = (0, 2y)

¥n

Slope of the tangent,

¥n

$${{dy} ¥¥over {dx}} = {{2y - 0} ¥¥over {0 - 2x}}$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {{dy} ¥¥over {dx}} = - {y ¥¥over x}$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow x¥¥,dy + y¥¥,dx = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow d(xy) = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow xy = c$$

¥n

This curve goes through point (3, 2). So this point satisfy the equation.

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ 3 . 2 = c¥n

$$¥¥Rightarrow$$ c = 6

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ Equation of the curve,

¥n

xy = 6

content: If the equation of one tangent to the circle with centre at (2, $$-$$1) from the origin is 3x + y = 0, then the equation of the other tangent through the origin is
explanation:
Length of perpendicular from the centre of circle on tangent = radius of circle.

¥n

$$r = ¥¥left| {{{3{x_1} + {y_1}} ¥¥over {¥¥sqrt {{{(3)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} }}} ¥¥right| ¥¥Rightarrow r = {5 ¥¥over {¥¥sqrt {10} }} = ¥¥sqrt {{5 ¥¥over 2}} $$

¥n

Equation of circle $${(x - b)^2} + {(y - k)^2} = {r^2}(h,b)$$

¥n

Centre points

¥n

$${(x - 2)^2} + {(y + 1)^2} = {¥¥left( {¥¥sqrt {{5 ¥¥over 2}} } ¥¥right)^2}$$

¥n

$${x^2} + 4 - 4x + {y^2} + 1 + 2y = {5 ¥¥over 2}$$

¥n

Let the equation of other tangent which is passing through origin is $$y = mx$$

¥n

$${x^2} + 4 - 4x + {m^2}{x^2} + 1 + 2mx = {5 ¥¥over 2}$$ [$$¥¥because$$ $$y = mx$$]

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {x^2}(1 + {m^2}) + (2m - 4)x + {{10 - 5} ¥¥over 2} = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow (1 + {m^2}){x^2} + (2m - 4)x + {5 ¥¥over 2} = 0$$

¥n

Compare with $$a{x^2} + bx + c = 0$$, where

¥n

$$a = (1 + {m^2}),b = 2m - 4$$ and $$c = {5 ¥¥over 2}$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {(2m - 4)^2} - 4(1 + {m^2}) ¥¥times {5 ¥¥over 2} = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow 4{m^2} + 16 - 16m - 4 ¥¥times {5 ¥¥over 2} - 4{m^2} ¥¥times {5 ¥¥over 2} = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow 4{m^2} - 16m + 16 - 10 - 10{m^2} = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow - 6{m^2} - 16m + 6 = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow - 2(3{m^2} + 8m - 3) = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow (3m - 1)(m + 3) = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow 3m - 1 = 0,¥¥,m + 3 = 0$$

¥n

$$m = {1 ¥¥over 3};m = - 3$$

¥n

(i) $$m = - 3,y = - 3x,3x + y = 0$$

¥n

(ii) $$m = {1 ¥¥over 3},y = {1 ¥¥over 3}x,3y - x = 0$$

¥n

Other tangent $$3y - x = 0$$

¥n

$$x - 3y = 0$$

content: The side AB of $$¥¥Delta$$ABC is fixed and is of length 2a unit. The vertex moves in the plane such that the vertical angle is always constant and is $$¥¥alpha$$. Let x-axis be along AB and the origin be at A. Then the locus of the vertex is
explanation:
>

¥n

Now, from $$¥¥Delta$$ACE

¥n

$$r¥¥sin ¥¥alpha = a$$

¥n

$$r = a¥¥cos ec¥¥,¥¥alpha $$

¥n

$$¥¥Rightarrow$$ Now, again in $$¥¥Delta$$ACE

¥n

$$CE = r¥¥cos ¥¥alpha = (a¥¥cos ec¥¥,¥¥alpha )¥¥cos ¥¥alpha = a¥¥cot ¥¥alpha $$

¥n

Now locus of the vertex will be a circle with radius $$r = a¥¥cos ec¥¥,¥¥alpha $$ and centre $$C(a,a¥¥cot ¥¥alpha )$$

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ Required locus is

¥n

$${(x - a)^2} + {(y - a¥¥cot ¥¥alpha )^2} = {a^2}¥¥cos e{c^2}¥¥alpha $$

¥n

$${x^2} + {a^2} - 2ax + {y^2} + {a^2}{¥¥cot ^2}¥¥alpha - 2ay¥¥cot ¥¥alpha = {a^2}¥¥cos e{c^2}¥¥alpha $$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} - 2ax - 2ay¥¥cot ¥¥alpha = 0$$

content: Two circles $${S_1} = p{x^2} + p{y^2} + 2g'x + 2f'y + d = 0$$ and $${S_2} = {x^2} + {y^2} + 2gx + 2fy + d' = 0$$ have a common chord PQ. The equation of PQ is
explanation:
We know that,

¥n

The equation of common chord of two circles $$S=x^2+y^2+2gx+2fy+c=0$$ and $$S'=x^2+y^2+2g'x+2f'y+c'=0$$ is

¥n

$$2x(g-g')+2y(f-f')+c-c'=0$$

¥n

i.e. $$S-S'=0$$

content: A straight line meets the co-ordinate axes at A and B. A circle is circumscribed about the triangle OAB, O being the origin. If m and n are the distances of the tangent to the circle at the origin from the points A and B respectively, the diameter of the circle is
explanation:
Let the line be $${x ¥¥over a} + {y ¥¥over b} = 1$$

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ A is (a, 0), B is (0, b).

¥n

Also, O is (0, 0).

¥n

$$¥¥therefore$$ Equation of circumcircle to $$¥¥Delta$$OAB is

¥n

$${x^2} + {y^2} - ax - by = 0$$

¥n

Tangent at (0, 0) to the circle is

¥n

$$x¥¥,.¥¥,0 + y¥¥,.¥¥,0 + {a ¥¥over 2}(x + 0) + {b ¥¥over 2}(y + 0) = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow ax + by = 0$$

¥n

$$m = {{a¥¥,.¥¥,a + b¥¥,.¥¥,0} ¥¥over {¥¥sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }} = {{{a^2}} ¥¥over {¥¥sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }}$$

¥n

$$n = {{a¥¥,.¥¥,0 + b¥¥,.¥¥,b} ¥¥over {¥¥sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }} = {{{b^2}} ¥¥over {¥¥sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }}$$

¥n

$$m + n = {{{a^2} + {b^2}} ¥¥over {¥¥sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} }} = ¥¥sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} $$

¥n

Radius of circle $$ = {{¥¥sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} } ¥¥over 2}$$

¥n

Hence, diameter of circle is $$m + n$$.

content: Two tangents to the circle x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = 4 at the points A and B meet at M($$-$$4, 0). The area of the quadrilateral MAOB, where O is the origin is
explanation:
>
Given, equation of circle x2 + y2 = 4

Centre = (0, 0) and radius = 2

$$¥¥therefore$$ $$MA = ¥¥sqrt {{{(4)}^2} - {{(2)}^2}} = ¥¥sqrt {12} = 2¥¥sqrt 3 $$

$$¥¥therefore$$ Area of quadrilateral MAOB

= 2 $$¥¥times$$ area of $$¥¥Delta$$MAO

$$ = 2 ¥¥times {1 ¥¥over 2}$$ $$¥¥times$$ MA $$¥¥times$$ OA

$$ = 2¥¥sqrt 3 $$ $$¥¥times$$ 2

$$ = 4¥¥sqrt 3 $$ sq. units.

content: The locus of the centre of the circles which touch both the circles x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = a<sup>2</sup> and x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = 4ax externally is
explanation:
Let (h, k) be the centre and r be the radius of the variable circle.

Since, variable circle touches the circle x2 + y2 = a2

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ $$¥¥sqrt {{h^2} + {k^2}} = a$$ ....(i)

Also variable circle touches the circle x2 + y2 = 4ax

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ $$¥¥sqrt {{{({h^2} + 2a)}^2} + {k^2}} = 2a + r$$ ....(ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$¥¥sqrt {{{({h^2} + 2a)}^2} + {k^2}} - ¥¥sqrt {{h^2} + {k^2}} = a$$

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ Locus of centre of variable circle is

$$¥¥sqrt {{{(x - 2a)}^2} + {y^2}} - ¥¥sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} = a$$

which represents the hyperbola.

content: The equation of circle of radius $$¥¥sqrt {17} $$ unit, with centre on the positive side of X-axis and through the point (0, 1) is
explanation:
Given, the radius of circle = $$¥¥sqrt {17} $$

With centre on positive sides of X-axis

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ Equation of circle is $${(x - a)^2} + {y^2} = 17$$

Since, it passes through (0, 1)

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ a2 + 1 = 17

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow $$ a = 4

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ Equation of circle is

$${x^2} + {y^2} - 8x - 1 = 0$$

content: A variable circle passes through the fixed point A(p, q) and touches X-axis. The locus of the other end of the diameter through A is
explanation:
In a circle, AB is a diameter where the coordinate of A is (p, q) and let the coordinate of B(x1, y1).

Equation of circle in diameter form is

$$(x - p)(x - {x_1}) + (y - q)(y - {y_1}) = 0$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {x^2} - (p + {x_1})x + p{x_1} + {y^2} - ({y_1} + q)y + q{y_1} = 0$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {x^2} - (p + {x_1})x + {y^2} - ({y_1} + q)y + p{x_1} + q{y_1} = 0$$

Since, this circle touches X - axis

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ y = 0

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {x^2} - (p + {x_1})x + p{x_1} + q{y_1} = 0$$

Also, the discriminant of above equation will be equal to zero because circle touches X-axis.

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ $${(p + {x_1})^2} = 4(p{x_1} + q{y_1})$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {p^2} + x_1^2 + 2p{x_1} = 4p{x_1} + 4q{y_1}$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow x_1^2 - 2p{x_1} + {p^2} = 4q{y_1}$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {({x_1} - p)^2} = 4q{y_1}$$

Therefore, the locus of point B is

$${(x - p)^2} = 4qy$$

content: If P(0, 0), Q(1, 0) and R$$¥¥left( {{1 ¥¥over 2},{{¥¥sqrt 3 } ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)$$ are three given points, then the centre of the circle for which the lines PQ, QR and RP are the tangents is
explanation: $$I = ¥¥left( {{{a{x_1} + b{x_2} + c{x_3}} ¥¥over {a + b + c}},{{a{y_1} + b{y_2} + c{y_3}} ¥¥over {a + b + c}}} ¥¥right)$$

content: Without changing the direction of the axes, the origin is transferred to the point (2, 3). Then the equation x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ 4x $$-$$ 6y + 9 = 0 changes to
explanation:
Put x = x' + 2

and y = y' + 3

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ x2 + y2 $$-$$ 4x $$-$$ 6y + 9 = 0

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow $$ (x' + 2)2 + (y' + 3)2 $$-$$ 4(x' + 2) $$-$$ 6(y' + 3) + 9 = 0

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow $$ x'2 + 4 + 4x' + y'2 + 9 + 6y' $$-$$ 4x' $$-$$ 8 $$-$$ 6y' $$-$$ 18 + 9 = 0

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow $$ x'2 + y'2 $$-$$ 4 = 0

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow $$ x2 + y2 = 4

content: The angle between a pair of tangents drawn from a point P to the circle x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> + 4x $$-$$ 6y + 9sin<sup>2</sup>$$¥¥alpha$$ + 13cos<sup>2</sup>$$¥¥alpha$$ = 0 is 2$$¥¥alpha$$. The equation of the locus of the point P is
explanation:
We have equation of circle x2 + y2 + 4x $$-$$ 6y + 9sin2$$¥¥alpha$$ + 13cos2$$¥¥alpha$$ = 0

Here, C $$ ¥¥equiv $$ ($$-$$ 2, 3)

Radius = $$¥¥sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{(3)}^2} - (9{{¥¥sin }^2}¥¥alpha + 13{{¥¥cos }^2}¥¥alpha )} $$

$$ = ¥¥sqrt {4 + 9 - 9{{¥¥sin }^2}¥¥alpha - 13{{¥¥cos }^2}¥¥alpha } $$

$$ = ¥¥sqrt {13 - 13(1 - {{¥¥sin }^2}¥¥alpha ) - 9{{¥¥sin }^2}¥¥alpha } $$

$$ = ¥¥sqrt {13{{¥¥sin }^2}¥¥alpha - 9{{¥¥sin }^2}¥¥alpha } $$

$$ = ¥¥sqrt {4{{¥¥sin }^2}¥¥alpha } = 2¥¥sin ¥¥alpha $$

>
Here, $$¥¥sin ¥¥alpha = {{AC} ¥¥over {PC}}$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow PC¥¥sin ¥¥alpha = AC$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow P{C^2}{¥¥sin ^2}¥¥alpha = A{C^2} = {(2¥¥sin ¥¥alpha )^2}$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow [{(h + 2)^2} + {(k - 3)^2}]{¥¥sin ^2}¥¥alpha = 4{¥¥sin ^2}¥¥alpha $$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {(h + 2)^2} + {(k - 3)^2} = 4$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {h^2} + 4 + 4h + {k^2} + 9 - 6k = 4$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {h^2} + {k^2} + 4h - 6k + 9 = 0$$

Hence, locus of a point is

$${x^2} + {y^2} + 4x - 6y + 9 = 0$$

content: If one of the diameter of the circle, given by the equation x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> + 4x + 6y $$-$$ 12 = 0, is a chord of a circle S, whose centre is (2, $$-$$ 3), the radius of S is
explanation:
Given, equation of circle is x2 + y2 + 4x + 6y $$-$$ 12 = 0

Centre C($$-$$ 2, $$-$$ 3) and radius = $$¥¥sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{( - 3)}^2} + 12} = 5$$

content: A chord AB is drawn from the point A(0, 3) on the circle x<sup>2</sup> + 4x + (y $$-$$ 3)<sup>2</sup> = 0, and is extended to M such that AM = 2AB. The locus of M is
explanation:
>
$$ ¥¥Rightarrow $$ B is mid-point of AM.

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ Coordinate of B is $$¥¥left( {{{0 + {x_1}} ¥¥over 2},{{3 + {y_1}} ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)$$

$$ = ¥¥left( {{{{x_1}} ¥¥over 2},{{{y_1} + 3} ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)$$

Since, B lies on the circle x2 + 4x + (y $$-$$ 3)2 = 0

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ $${¥¥left( {{{{x_1}} ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)^2} + 4¥¥left( {{{{x_1}} ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right) + {¥¥left( {{{{y_1} + 3} ¥¥over 2} - 3} ¥¥right)^2} = 0$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {{x_1^2} ¥¥over 4} + 2{x_1} + {¥¥left( {{{{y_1} - 3} ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)^2} = 0$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {{x_1^2} ¥¥over 4} + 2{x_1} + {{y_1^2 + 9 - 6{y_1}} ¥¥over 4} = 0$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow x_1^2 + y_1^2 + 8{x_1} - 6{y_1} + 9 = 0$$

Hence, locus of a point is $${x^2} + {y^2} + 8x - 6y + 9 = 0$$

content: Let A be the centre of the circle $${x^2} + {y^2} - 2x - 4y - 20 = 0$$. Let B(1, 7) and D(4, $$-$$2) be two points on the circle such that tangents at B and D meet at C. The area of the quadrilateral ABCD is
explanation:
Given, equation of circle is

$${x^2} + {y^2} - 2x - 4y - 20 = 0$$

Center (1, 2) and radius = $$¥¥sqrt {{{(1)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + 20} = 5$$

>
Coordinate of intersecting point of tangents at B and D is C (16, 7).

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ Area of quadrilateral ABCD

$$ = 2 ¥¥times ar(¥¥Delta ABC)$$

$$ = 2 ¥¥times {1 ¥¥over 2} ¥¥times 15 ¥¥times 5 = 75$$ sq units

content: The common chord of the circles  $${x^2} + {y^2} - 4x - 4y = 0$$ and $$2{x^2} + 2{y^2} = 32$$ subtends at the origin an angle equal to
explanation:
Given, equation of circles are $${x^2} + {y^2} - 4x - 4y = 0$$ and $$2{x^2} + 2{y^2} = 32$$ or $${x^2} + {y^2} - 4x - 4y = 0$$ and $${x^2} + {y^2} = 16$$

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ Equation of common chord is

$$({x^2} + {y^2} - 4x - 4y) - ({x^2} + {y^2} - 16) = 0$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow - 4x - 4y + 16 = 0$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow x + y = 4$$

This common chord passes through (2, 2), i.e. centre of first circle.

Also, (0, 0) is at the circumference of the first circle.

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ Common chord will subtent $${¥¥pi ¥¥over 2}$$ angle at (0, 0).

content: The locus of the mid-points of the chords of the circle $${x^2} + {y^2} + 2x - 2y - 2 = 0$$, which make an angle of 90$$^¥¥circ$$ at the centre is
explanation:
Given, equation of circle is

$${x^2} + {y^2} + 2x - 2y - 2 = 0$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {(x + 1)^2} + {(y - 1)^2} = 4$$

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ Centre ($$-$$1, 1) and radius = 2

Let (h, k) be the mid-point of chord.

>
From figure,

$$OP = ¥¥sqrt {{{(h + 1)}^2} + {{(k - 1)}^2}} $$

In $$¥¥Delta OAP$$

$$¥¥sin 45^¥¥circ = {{OP} ¥¥over {OA}}$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {1 ¥¥over {¥¥sqrt 2 }} = {{¥¥sqrt {{{(h + 1)}^2} + {{(k - 1)}^2}} } ¥¥over 2}$$

On squaring both sides, we get

$${(h + 1)^2} + {(k - 1)^2} = 2$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {h^2} + {k^2} + 2h - 2k = 0$$

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ Locus of P will be

$${x^2} + {y^2} + 2x - 2y = 0$$

content: If one of the diameters of the curve x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> $$-$$ 4x $$-$$ 6y + 9 = 0 is a chord of a circle with centre (1, 1), the radius of this circle is
The equation of a line parallel to the line 3x + 4y = 0 and touching the circle x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = 9 in the first quadrant, is
explanation:
Equation of circle is x2 + y2 = 9 ....(i)

And equation of line is 3x + 4y = 0 .... (i)

Equation of line parallel to the line (ii),

3x + 4y = k

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow y = {{ - 3x} ¥¥over 4} + {k ¥¥over 4}$$ .... (iii)

$$ ¥¥because $$ Circle touches the line (iii), so by the condition of tangency,

$${c^2} = {a^2}(1 + {m^2})$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {¥¥left( {{k ¥¥over 4}} ¥¥right)^2} = 9¥¥left[ {1 + {{¥¥left( {{{ - 3} ¥¥over 4}} ¥¥right)}^2}} ¥¥right]$$

$$ ¥¥because $$ $$¥¥left[ {c = {k ¥¥over 4},a = 3,m = {{ - 3} ¥¥over 4}} ¥¥right]$$

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow k = ¥¥pm 15$$

>

Hence, 3x + 4y = 15 touches the circle in the first quadrant.

content: The locus of the mid-points of chords of the circle x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = 1, which subtends a right angle at the origin, is
explanation:
Let (h, k) be the coordinates of the mid-point of a chord, which subtends a right angle at the origin.

Then, equation of the chord is

hx + ky $$-$$ 1 = h2 + k2 $$-$$ 1 [using T = S']

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow $$ hx + ky = h2 + k2

The combined equation of the pair of lines joining the origin to the points of intersection of x2 + y2 = 1 and hx + ky = h2 + k2 is

$${x^2} + {y^2} - 1{¥¥left( {{{hx + ky} ¥¥over {{h^2} + {k^2}}}} ¥¥right)^2} = 0$$

Line given by the above equation are at right angle. Therefore, coefficient of x2 + coefficient of y2 = 0

i.e. h2 + k2 = $${1 ¥¥over 2}$$

$$ ¥¥therefore $$ x2 + y2 = $${1 ¥¥over 2}$$

content: Twenty metres of wire is available to fence off a flower bed in the form of a circular sector. What must the radius of the circle be, if the area of the flower bed be greatest?
explanation:
Perimeter of sector = 20 m

¥n

$${¥¥theta ¥¥over {360^¥¥circ }}2¥¥pi r + 2r = 20$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {¥¥theta ¥¥over {360^¥¥circ }}¥¥pi r + r = 10$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {¥¥theta ¥¥over {360^¥¥circ }} = {{10 - r} ¥¥over {¥¥pi r}}$$

¥n

We know that, area of sector $$A = {¥¥theta ¥¥over {360^¥¥circ }}¥¥pi {r^2}$$

¥n

$$A = {{10 - r} ¥¥over {¥¥pi r}}¥¥,.¥¥,¥¥pi {r^2}$$

¥n

$$A = (10 - r)r$$

¥n

$${{dA} ¥¥over {dr}} = {d ¥¥over {dr}}(10r - {r^2})$$

¥n

$$ = 10 - 2r$$

¥n

For maximum area

¥n

$${{dA} ¥¥over {dr}} = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow 10 - 2r = 0$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow r = 5$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {{{d^2}A} ¥¥over {d{r^2}}} = {d ¥¥over {dr}}(10 - 2r) = - 2$$

¥n

$$ ¥¥Rightarrow {{{d^2}A} ¥¥over {d{r^2}}} < 0$$

¥n

Maximum value of $$r = 5$$ m

content: Let P be a variable point on a circle C and Q be a fixed point outside C. If R is the midpoint of the line segment PQ, then locus of R is

options.content: a circle and a pair of straight lines

explanation:
Let the equation of circle is

(x $$-$$ $$¥¥alpha$$)2 + ($$¥¥gamma$$ $$-$$ $$¥¥beta$$)2 = r2

$$¥¥therefore$$ x = $$¥¥alpha$$ + r cos$$¥¥theta$$

y = $$¥¥beta$$ + r sin$$¥¥theta$$

$$¥¥therefore$$ P $$ ¥¥equiv $$ ($$¥¥alpha$$ + r cos$$¥¥theta$$, $$¥¥beta$$ + r sin$$¥¥theta$$)

$$¥¥theta$$ = (a, b)

Let R = (h, k)

$$¥¥therefore$$ $$h = {{¥¥alpha + r¥¥cos ¥¥theta } ¥¥over 2},k = {{¥¥beta + r¥¥sin ¥¥theta } ¥¥over 2}$$

$$r¥¥cos ¥¥theta = 2h - ¥¥alpha $$ and $$r¥¥sin ¥¥theta = 2k - ¥¥beta $$

$$¥¥therefore$$ $${r^2}({¥¥cos ^2}¥¥theta + {¥¥sin ^2}¥¥theta ) = {(2h - ¥¥alpha )^2} + {(2k - ¥¥beta )^2}$$

$$ = {¥¥left( {h - {¥¥alpha ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)^2} + ¥¥left( {k - {¥¥beta ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right) = {¥¥left( {{r ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)^2}$$

Locus of R is $${¥¥left( {x - {¥¥alpha ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)^2} + {¥¥left( {y - {¥¥beta ¥¥over 2}} ¥¥right)^2} = {{{r^2}} ¥¥over 4}$$

Which represents a circle.
